response: The EU’s GDPR, in effect since May 2018, reshaped personal data handling by applying to any organization processing EU residents’ data, regardless of location. It grants rights such as data access, erasure, portability, and explicit consent requirements, mandates breach reporting within 72 hours, and can impose fines of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million. Major penalties include Meta’s €1.2 billion fine in 2023, Amazon’s €746 million fine, and Google fines totaling over €200 million. While criticized for compliance burdens, cookie-banner frustration, and uneven enforcement, GDPR has established privacy as a fundamental right and influenced laws such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act.
